critic_raw:
The methodology is well-structured and clearly written. I will work through the specific areas where I believe genuine methodological problems exist, ordered by root cause.

---

**Issue Group 1: Label Construction Creates a Structural Dependency on Ticket-History Features**

The positive label is defined as 'alert linked to an incident-response ticket within 24 hours.' Ticket-history features are defined as 'count and summary statistics of incident-response tickets linked to the same host/user/IP in the prior 14 days.' These two quantities share a common generative process: an entity that has a ticket in the prior 14 days is precisely the kind of entity most likely to generate a ticket in the next 24 hours, not because of true threat persistence, but because of analyst workflow artifacts — once a case is open, analysts create follow-on tickets for the same entities. The 'metadata leakage check' acknowledges this risk but only promises to validate that ticket features 'do not inadvertently correlate with the label due to label-data construction artifacts.' That framing understates the problem. The correlation may not be accidental: it may be structural and mechanistically caused by the same analyst deciding both that an entity is worth a ticket today (generating the label) and that it was worth a ticket last week (generating the feature). This is not future leakage in the strict temporal sense — the ticket-history window predates the alert — but it is label-generating-process leakage: the feature is a proxy for 'an analyst is actively working this entity,' which is also the dominant cause of the label. The ablation study proposed (dropping ticket-history features) would confirm or deny this, but only if the entity graph features do not absorb the same signal. If entity graph features also reflect recent ticket activity via the shared entity store, the ablation result is ambiguous. Evidence one way: compute the mutual information between ticket-history features and the label separately on entities with active open tickets vs. entities with closed or absent tickets. If the predictive lift from ticket features is concentrated entirely in the 'active open case' stratum, the feature is capturing analyst workflow state, not threat signal.

---

**Issue Group 2: The Validation Set Is Proportionally Oversized, Distorting the Temporal Experiment**

The split is months 1–14 (training, 58%), months 15–17 (validation, 25%), months 18–24 (test, 25%). The validation set is three months and the test set is seven months, yet both are labeled as '25%' — this is an arithmetic inconsistency (3 months ≈ 12.5% of 24, not 25%). More importantly, the described split means the validation and test sets are roughly equal in calendar size despite the test set being described as larger. The actual percentages depend on alert volume per month, which is not stated, so the 25%/25% claim may be based on alert counts rather than calendar months. This is worth clarifying because: (a) if alert volume grew substantially over 24 months (common in growing SOC deployments), the test set is more densely populated than the validation set, making test nDCG estimates lower-variance but training-set underrepresentation a real concern; (b) if alert volume is roughly constant, then months 15–17 = ~12.5% and months 18–24 = ~29%, and the stated percentages are wrong, suggesting a possible data-handling error. Either way the discrepancy should be resolved. Evidence: examine the month-by-month alert volume distribution. If test-set months have significantly different alert rates than training months, volume-adjusted evaluation is needed.

---

**Issue Group 3: nDCG@10 as Primary Metric Assumes a Fixed Daily Alert Volume That May Not Match Production**

The justification for nDCG@10 is 'SOC analysts review ~10 alerts per shift.' If the actual daily alert volume presented to any one analyst is much larger than 10, then optimizing and reporting nDCG@10 understates the operational requirement. Conversely, if the ranker is being applied to a queue of 200 alerts per shift, an analyst acting on top-10 may be discarding most of the queue without any rank-based guidance, in which case nDCG@50 or nDCG@100 is the operative metric. The experiment does include nDCG@50 as a secondary metric, which partially addresses this, but the primary model selection criterion (nDCG@10) may select a model that is locally optimal at position 10 but worse at position 50 than the baseline. This is not a flaw in the evaluation of a held-out test set, but it is a flaw in model selection if the deployment scenario requires a different cutoff. Evidence: obtain the actual analyst queue-length distribution from the SOC ticketing system. If the median daily alert count per analyst is greater than 20, rerun hyperparameter selection using nDCG@20 or nDCG@50 and check whether the selected model changes.

---

**Issue Group 4: The Static Entity Store Is Not Temporally Isolated Per-Split**

Entity graph features are extracted 'from a static entity store (host–user–IP–rule relationships from the prior 30 days).' The word 'static' is ambiguous: does it mean the entity store was built once from all 24 months of data, or that it is re-built per-alert using only that alert's prior-30-day window? If the store was built once from the full dataset, entity relationship features for training-set alerts may incorporate entity co-occurrence information from validation or test periods. For example, a host that first appeared as a C2 beacon in month 16 (validation) would appear in the entity store with elevated connectivity even for month-12 (training) alerts if the store was built globally. The audit checks described focus on ticket-resolution dates and escalation outcomes, not on entity store construction. This is a potential form of future leakage that is neither addressed nor explicitly ruled out. Evidence: confirm in implementation that entity store snapshots are built using a rolling window that respects the alert timestamp, not a global retrospective build.
all_issues_raised: Ticket-history features may capture analyst workflow state (active open case) rather than threat signal, due to structural overlap between the label-generating process (IR ticket in next 24h) and the feature-generating process (IR tickets in prior 14 days on same entity), making observed lift from this feature ambiguous.

The stated 25%/25% validation/test split is arithmetically inconsistent with 3 vs. 7 calendar months, suggesting either a data-handling error or an undisclosed assumption about alert volume growth that is not validated.

Hyperparameter selection on nDCG@10 may select a suboptimal model if the true analyst queue exceeds 10 alerts per shift, and the operational cutoff is not empirically established before model selection.

The 'static entity store' description is ambiguous about whether it was built globally from all 24 months or per-alert using a rolling window; a globally-built store would leak future entity relationship information into training-set features.

The stated 25%/25% validation/test split is arithmetically inconsistent with 3 vs. 7 calendar months, suggesting either a data-handling error or an undisclosed assumption about alert volume growth that is not validated.

Hyperparameter selection on nDCG@10 may select a suboptimal model if the true analyst queue exceeds 10 alerts per shift, and the operational cutoff is not empirically established before model selection.

The 'static entity store' description is ambiguous about whether it was built globally from all 24 months or per-alert using a rolling window; a globally-built store would leak future entity relationship information into training-set features.
